chapters.title: Tiny chips, enormous power — and fragile supply
chapters.text:
Integrated circuits no larger than a grain of rice underpin smartphones, GPS, medical scanners, and modern warfare. They helped build Silicon Valley, fueled Asia's economic rise, and anchor U.S. military edge. Yet few industries concentrate so much capability in so few corporate hands, creating wealth and vulnerability in equal measure.

Silicon Valley took its name from the material that, together with billions of transistors, forms the heart of every chip.

Logic chips process information — the "brains" inside devices. Taiwan produced thirty-seven percent of global logic chips in 2019. Memory production leans heavily on two Korean giants supplying nearly half the market. Cutting-edge fabrication depends on extreme ultraviolet lithography machines made exclusively by the Dutch firm ASML.

In the age of AI, it's often said that data is the new oil. Yet the real limitation we face isn't the availability of data but of processing power. ~ Chris Miller, Prof.

Concentration yields profit but also risk. An earthquake near key fabs could stall advanced manufacturing worldwide. Natural disasters can be modeled and mitigated; geopolitical shocks are harder to predict. China's 2022 military drills around Taiwan sounded alarms far beyond the island, reminding planners how much commerce rests on calm across the strait.

How did semiconductors become both indispensable and precarious? How did China leap from Mao-era farm labor for scientists to serious high-tech competition? What levers keep the global balance from tipping? The chapters ahead trace those questions through decades of invention, offshoring, rivalry, and statecraft.

For example, logic chips process the data, being the brains of devices. As for memory chips, the world relies heavily on two Korean companies. Moreover, top-notch technology is impossible without extreme ultraviolet lithography machines. This distribution of resources provides mentioned countries with enormous profits but simultaneously poses serious risks, concentrating critical manufacturing in one area.

chapters.title: Inventing the chip was only half the struggle
chapters.text:
Mid-twentieth-century computers were room-sized and slow. Engineers turned to semiconductors — materials like silicon and germanium that conduct poorly until doped with phosphorus and electrified. In 1948 William Shockley demonstrated a three-layer switch, the transistor, replacing fragile vacuum tubes and shrinking machines dramatically.

A decade later Jack Kilby at Texas Instruments and Robert Noyce at Fairchild Semiconductor embedded multiple transistors on a single germanium or silicon die — the integrated circuit. Noyce and seven colleagues had defected from Shockley's lab; historians call them the "traitorous eight," founders of Silicon Valley culture and venture-backed innovation.

Early chips were prohibitively expensive until customers with deep pockets appeared. Sputnik's 1957 launch panicked Washington, perpetuating fear of falling behind the Soviet Union. Officials funneled defense contracts to Texas Instruments and Fairchild for guidance systems and Apollo-era computing. Military demand proved chips worked; consumer scale remained the dream engineers chased.

Photolithography — patented by engineer Jay Lathrop — etched finer patterns with light, shrinking dies and costs. When Pentagon budgets tightened, firms pivoted to commercial markets just as personal computing exploded and computer sales rocketed.

Fairchild's first civilian win was a hearing-aid chip for Zenith, originally designed for a NASA satellite — a symbol of defense spinoff becoming everyday technology.

Reaching the mass market became possible when electronics scientist Jay Lathrop patented photolithography. This method of using light to create small and detailed patterns in a material decreased the size of chips. So, when the government reduced funding, companies switched to the mass market, where computer sales rocketed. Engineers dreamed of putting integrated circuits in everyday products, and lithography made that economical.

chapters.title: Offshoring labor reshaped — and threatened — U.S. dominance
chapters.text:
Transistors and lithography initially kept America ahead. Its integrated circuits and computers outclassed rivals. Confidence bred complacency — and a push to cut costs by assembling abroad.

Fairchild opened Hong Kong plants where wages ran ten times lower; competitors mirrored the trend into Singapore, Korea, Malaysia, and Taiwan. It seemed like a win-win: Asian partners gained jobs — electronics approached ten percent of Singapore's GNP and a quarter of manufacturing employment — while U.S. firms gained price competitiveness. Hosting American factories also implied a security umbrella during the Cold War.

Geopolitics reinforced economics. Washington avoided punishing Japan's high-tech sector after World War II, helping Sony license transistor know-how. Interconnected economies became leverage for influence across allies.

Interconnections between countries' economies are an effective leverage for geopolitical influence.

Japan at first lagged in fabrication yet excelled at products built around chips — transistor radios, calculators, eventually the Walkman, one of history's most popular devices. State-backed cheap credit, government support, and protected domestic markets with quotas on American imports eroded U.S. share. By the 1980s American firms held just 1.7 percent of the DRAM memory market they invented — a pivotal alarm that Washington could not ignore.

Another reason for cooperation with Asian countries was geopolitical. Americans wanted to ensure the Soviet Union wouldn't obtain new allies during the Cold War. It was so crucial that the US even refused to ban Japan's high tech to punish the country for its role in World War II. This policy assisted Sony in getting the license to produce transistors.

The US didn't see Japan as a serious competitor at first, believing it couldn't make better chips than Americans. Indeed, Japan didn't initially excel in integrated circuits, but it became a leader in designing products that contain them. Cheap credit for companies, government support, and a protected national market with quotas on American products all contributed to Japan's rise.

chapters.title: How America clawed back from Japan's surge
chapters.text:
A 1989 essay collection by nationalist politician Shintaro Ishihara envisioned Japan using semiconductor strength to displace U.S. influence — economically and militarily, including building a powerful army free of American restrictions. Similar sentiments circulated in milder form among mainstream Japanese leaders. Americans immediately took measures to redress the balance.

Innovation accelerated. Lynn Conway and Carver Mead published design rules that automated chip layout. Micron challenged Japanese DRAM makers by shrinking die size and simplifying process steps to cut defects and save time.

Nowadays, the world relies on three U.S.-based companies — Cadence, Synopsys, and Mentor — specializing in chip design software.

Business models shifted. Intel, co-founded by Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore, abandoned DRAM manufacturing it could not scale against Japanese rivals and doubled down on microprocessors. IBM's PC contract made Intel's x86 architecture the personal-computer standard and established a lucrative monopoly.

Production also migrated to Korea and Taiwan to undercut Japanese share. Samsung exploited the rivalry, licensing Micron's DRAM advances while officials backed cheap credit.

U.S. recovery benefited from Japanese missteps too: Toshiba ignored Fujio Masuoka's flash-memory breakthrough while Intel commercialized NAND storage, and Japanese firms underestimated PCs. America led global semiconductor shipments by 1993; Korea rose to second within five years.

Another crucial step for American companies was moving more facilities to Korea and Taiwan, where the workforce was cheap. This way, the USA wanted to weaken the Japanese market share. Korea's firm Samsung made the most of this American-Japanese rivalry. For instance, they bought a license for designing cutting-edge DRAM from Micron.

These steps helped the US to improve its position in the market. At the same time, its success also stemmed from the failures of Japanese firms. With huge sales, they neglected innovation. Japan's most severe mistake was ignoring PCs, ceding the personal-computer revolution to American software and chip makers.

chapters.title: ASML and TSMC rose while Intel clung to old monopolies
chapters.text:
Shrinking transistors required extreme ultraviolet lithography — a hurdle American toolmaker GCA failed despite early leadership, undone more by poor management than by Japanese rivals Canon and Nikon. Dutch ASML seized the opening with global component sourcing, extraordinary engineering talent, and geopolitical neutrality that attracted billions from Intel in 2012 and similar backing from Samsung.

A EUV machine introduced by ASML comprised hundreds of thousands of precision parts shipped from suppliers worldwide. Neutral Netherlands location reassured investors across continents.

Korea leveraged DRAM licenses, state support, and cheap credit. Taiwan moved differently. Minister K. T. Li recruited former Texas Instruments executive Morris Chang to launch TSMC with roughly half government funding and private capital from local industrialists "asked" to contribute.

Chang's foundry model — manufacture designs others create — removed the capital barrier of owning fabs. TSMC became the indispensable partner for firms without fabrication plants, embodying his dream of a dedicated manufacturing ally.

Intel, meanwhile, rested on x86 dominance, passed on manufacturing Apple's iPhone chips as unprofitable, and stumbled combining design with leading-edge production. It under-invested in mobile and AI even while funding EUV research. American leadership looked fragile again as China joined the race for leadership in integrated circuits.

Meanwhile, Intel's role weakened. Relying heavily on the x86 chips monopoly, it ignored new spheres, like mobile phones and artificial intelligence. Moreover, combining manufacturing and designing was challenging, which influenced the quality of products. Consequently, Intel didn't make the most of EUV, despite its massive investments in it. Intel could have made chips for iPhones but declined Steve Jobs's offer, considering it unprofitable — a decision that looks catastrophic in hindsight.

The dominance in the chip industry rests significantly on innovation. Using extreme ultraviolet light to make integrated circuits smaller would be a huge advantage. But it was an unattainable aim for American lithography toolmakers. GCA, a top company at the beginning of the 1980s, had lost its leadership. The main reason was poor management, not rivalry with Japanese firms like Canon and Nikon.

chapters.title: China's ascent leaned on Western technology
chapters.text:
Under Mao, science was starved and foreign inputs banned; by 1975 only one in a thousand Chinese chips functioned reliably. Market opening reversed isolation but deepened import dependence — until Beijing weaponized scale.

U.S. and UK chip architectures and designs as well as Taiwanese foundries have played a central role in the development of China's supercomputer programs. ~ Chris Miller, Prof.

Market access bought technology transfers: Qualcomm partnered with Huaxintong; IBM sold stakes and shared know-how in 2015 when Chinese sales plunged twenty percent. These policies contributed to Huawei's rise alongside immense governmental support, strategy focused on quality at lower price, Western expert advice, towering R&D budgets, and leadership in cell-tower equipment.

Although Huawei earned less than a billion dollars in 1999, its expenditure on consultants was fifty million — a staggering ratio showing how seriously leadership invested in capability.

Espionage allegations shadowed Huawei's rise. The Jinhua case was stark: the firm stole Micron files, patented them domestically, and convinced Chinese courts to ban Micron products — triggering U.S. export restrictions that crippled Jinhua.

These policies contributed to the rise of Huawei. Sometimes the price for market access was trading of technology — that's what IBM did in 2015, striving to improve sales since they dropped by 20% in China. Moreover, IBM sold almost all its stakes in its facilities in China and Malaysia to Chinese firms.

Nevertheless, Huawei's reputation was dubious. Experts accused the company of spying on behalf of the Chinese government. These close ties between private firms and the state became a severe concern to American officials.

China turned its dependence on foreign integrated circuits into an advantage by using its market as leverage for influence. Huawei's success also stemmed from immense governmental support, strategy focused on building commodities with better quality and at a lower price, Western experts' recommendations, a tremendous R&D budget, and leadership in providing equipment for cell towers.

Another warning bell was the story of the Chinese company Jinhua, which stole confidential Micron files and patented these inventions in China. When the US sued Jinhua, it did the same in a local court, accusing Micron of violating their intellectual property. Chinese judges found the American company guilty.

Did you know? Think tank MacroPolo estimated nearly one-third of top AI researchers are Chinese nationals.

chapters.title: Taiwan sits at the center of every strategic calculation
chapters.text:
U.S. chipmakers depended on Chinese revenue yet downplayed the tension in policy debates, preferring normalized trade that preserved profits. State-subsidized Chinese competitors pursued goals beyond shareholder returns. Australia, Japan, France, and others eventually restricted Huawei 5G gear over security fears after experts highlighted risks.

Huawei matured into a telecom powerhouse designing advanced smartphone silicon and matching Google-scale R&D, even as bribery accusations circulated abroad. U.S. sanctions over Iran violations activated America's asymmetric weapons: dominance in design software, essential ASML components, and influence over Samsung and TSMC fabs protected by U.S. security guarantees.

By 2020 Huawei could not source chips built with American tools — throttling its flagship devices and demonstrating how supply-chain law became geopolitics.

Apple and Huawei have been the main customers of TSMC for years. Yet the 2022 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan remind us how fragile the power balance is.

Export controls spurred Chinese investment in open RISC-V architectures and domestic NAND capacity, yet advanced logic still flows through Taiwan. Even Intel relies on TSMC for leading-edge nodes too costly to duplicate quickly in either Washington or Beijing, keeping the island at the choke point of global technology.

Although American companies became dependent on the Chinese market, they hardly mentioned it in conversations with the government. Instead, they tried to normalize the state of affairs and save their profits. Yet the fact that their fiercest competitor provided their biggest market posed many risks, not only economical. Chinese firms rested on state financing, which made them tools to reach geopolitical goals.

On the other hand, American restrictions encouraged China to invest even more in high-tech; China supported research on RISC-V, a new architecture to weaken the American monopoly on x86 chips. Chinese companies have a good chance of a share in the NAND market and modern logic chips. Yet they still depend on the US and, most importantly, Taiwan.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Gordon Moore's 1965 forecast — transistor counts doubling roughly every two years, driving down cost — sounded fantastical yet proved prescient. Chips permeate modern living. Fabrication plants produced thirteen percent more integrated circuits in 2021 than in 2020, yet pandemic-era remote work still outpaced supply, exposing brittle chains across automakers, game consoles, and medical devices.

Despite its crucial role, the chip industry rests on a few key players whose interconnections present risks but simultaneously constitute leverage to maintain balance. A small shift may change the global economy overnight.

Innovation remains the survival strategy. Near-term developments may include novel tube-shaped transistors replacing some 3D structures, Jim Keller's claimed fifty-fold density gains, custom accelerators outpacing general-purpose CPUs, and cheaper AI deployment as hardware improves — advances many big tech firms already prototype internally.

How society absorbs these leaps is still unfolding — reality may outpace science fiction sooner than expected. Companies know that harnessing innovations is the only way to save or improve their positions in the market. The latter is critical: a shift may change the global economy. We live in an exciting time when researchers may outpace science fiction writers.

Try this

• Follow reputable tech journalism weekly to track semiconductor news.
• Identify which chips power your devices, who designed them, and where they were fabricated.
• Watch a TED talk on the future of microelectronics.
